preFix.title: White bean and rosemary soup
preFix.subtitle: Thick, creamy, and deeply Italian
preFix.excerpt: Cannellini beans blended with rosemary-scented olive oil, garlic, and stock into a thick, warming soup. Finished with a drizzle of good olive oil. Simple enough to make on a weeknight, good enough for a dinner party.
preFix.sourceNotes: White bean and rosemary soup (ribollita di fagioli) is a Tuscan staple. This simplified version uses tinned cannellini beans rather than dried, following the home-cook approach widely published from the 1990s onwards.

content.text: The key technique in this soup is

the rosemary in the olive oil before anything else goes into the pot. The rosemary's flavour compounds dissolve into the fat and carry through the entire soup. Skip this step and the rosemary tastes like a herb that happened to be nearby; do it properly and the soup tastes as though it has been cooking for hours. Tinned cannellini beans make this a 25-minute soup; the result is one of the most satisfying quick meals in the Italian repertoire.
Infuse the rosemary and build the base

Pour {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil into a large saucepan over a low heat. Add the {{rosemary}} of rosemary sprigs and let them warm gently in the oil for 2 minutes until fragrant. Add the {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for another 2 minutes without browning.
Remove the rosemary sprigs. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{celery}} celery to the pan. Cook over a medium-low heat for 8 minutes until soft.
Add the {{cannellini-beans}} of drained and rinsed cannellini beans and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Simmer for 10 minutes.

Use a stick blender to blend about half the soup, leaving some beans whole for texture. Alternatively, scoop half the soup into a blender, blend smooth, and return to the pot. Stir to combine. Taste and season.
Ladle into bowls. Drizzle with good olive oil. Add grated Parmesan if using.

items.fix: The oil should be warm but not sizzling when the rosemary goes in, and the infusing should take at least 2 full minutes. The kitchen should smell strongly of rosemary before the garlic is added.
items.cause: The rosemary was added to cold oil and heated too briefly.

items.fix: Blend a larger proportion of the beans, or mash some against the side of the pot with the back of a spoon. Both thicken without adding stock.
items.cause: Tinned cannellini beans have less starch than dried beans cooked from scratch.

items.fix: Keep the heat very low when cooking the garlic. It should turn golden and sweet, not brown. Sliced garlic cooks more evenly than crushed here.
items.cause: The garlic was added to hot oil and cooked too quickly.

items.fix: Season at every stage. Add a small splash of lemon juice at the end to lift the flavour. A generous drizzle of good olive oil at serving makes a significant difference.
items.cause: Cannellini beans need generous seasoning and the stock must be well-flavoured.

content.text: White bean soups are a staple of Tuscan peasant cooking: cheap, filling, and made from storecupboard ingredients that last all winter. The pairing of cannellini beans with rosemary and olive oil is so standard in Tuscany that it barely needs a recipe. In Britain it became widely known through Italian cookbooks in the 1990s and has stayed as one of the most reliable quick winter soups.
